speaker: interviewer
text: --9, 1996. You were talking about how you got into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Philharmonic.

speaker: interviewer
text: --philharmonic

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Philharmonic, I-- I met other girls that we became friends. And it was very interesting work, because [INAUDIBLE] was a great pedagogue and a wonderful conductor. So he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he taught us while-- while singing professional. And when-- when we gave a concert, it was-- the choir sounded like-- like a symphonic choir. And there we got acquainted with all the girls. And we became friends. As a  matter of fact, next week is coming-- his wife. He passed away. It's already 15-- 15 years that he passed away. And his wife Rivka [INAUDIBLE] Weiner comes to visit me. She comes already the third or the fourth year. She's also a singer, and sings until today. And she appears-- when she comes here she appears for Jewish organizations like YIVO, Workmen's Circle. And she sings very nicely Jewish songs. And-- and then we-- we gave concerts all over Poland that belonged to the Soviet Union. And then came a new director from Kiev to our Philharmonic. And he heard the first time the choir. He fell in love with it. And he said that-- that such a choir has to be shown in the Soviet Union, what we have in the west, what we've found, and what work we are doing. So they arranged for the 17th of June, 1941 to go to Kiev to give the first concert. And then, if we will be successful in Kiev, they'll arrange a concert in every capital of the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, between 1939 and 1941, what was life like for you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life was normal. It wasn't so-- we couldn't-- while I-- you see, I became the provider for the family. Because I earned 400 rubles a month. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had my oldest brother, who was-- who knew wonderful the-- the Russian language in writing and in speaking. And he was of religious man. Three times a day he prayed. And he had a wife and four children. One of his sons was-- in the Belz of Yeshiva, he was an [INAUDIBLE]. And he passed away, David. David [INAUDIBLE]. He was really an [INAUDIBLE]. And he got work to be a manager of groceries in a small factory. And he had under him so many, so many people that he should provide groceries, products for-- for those and those people. He didn't know anything about-- about giving somebody something. And then came in a Russian officer with a-- with a high rank. And he said, Mr. Spitzman, I want a kilo of rice, a kilo of sugar, a kilo of that. And my-- my brother looks at him. And he said, I'm very sorry. I can't give you. I have so much, and so much, only for my people. Next day, he was taken away. He was put in prison. He was sent to Siberia. I haven't heard from him or seen him until today. That was the end of it. And-- and it wasn't only one example. But people have got to cope. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and they were people who were very nice. For instance, where we went, we went in places that-- that nobody could come in. Because we were artists. And we appeared for the military and for the-- for the-- and we came and we-- for instance, there was very hard to get sugar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You couldn't even dream of it. But where we went, there was a table full of cookies, of candies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took some. And that's how we helped ourselves. You see? And I was the provider of them. Until-- until we were sent off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before that, I brought my father to the philharmonic. And I gave them a permission that while I will be away, my father should get my monthly pay, payment. And they, besides when-- when we went out somewheres, they gave daily money for the day. For instance, how many days we've been out of Lutsk, they paid us daily, so much and so much. So I said, I'll cut-- for me it will be enough, the daily payment. And the rest should go to the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was paying you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The philharmonic. It was a government thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a government.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything that was arranged belonged to the government. There was no private enterprises whatsoever. It has got to be from the government. And whoever worked, wherever he worked, the government paid. And then we left. It was the 17th of June, '41. We left-- we left for Kiev. We gave the first concert. We were very successful, so they didn't let us go back. Although, we thought we are going for a few days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't even take with me a pair of shoes. My sister wanted to-- to give me another pair of shoes. I'll come back. Didn't take them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you all get to Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was arranged by the government. With a-- with a-- we went by train. But this was arranged by the government. We had no-- nothing to do. It's the management arranged it. And we got our forms. We-- we appeared in Ukrainian clothes, like on the stage as I showed you the picture. And then we-- from Kiev, we were sent to a city Rostov that lies on the River Don, also a very well-known city. And we gave-- there was a special [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] city-like or town for pilots. It was a pilot city in a special arrangement. And we gave there a concert. And it was a Saturday night. And next day was Sunday. And they wanted to reward us with something. And they took us to the opera. And there was given a-- a Ukrainian opera. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after the first act, a military man came out and said that Germany went to war with Soviet Union. The first city that was bombed was Lutsk. And then we were stuck. We couldn't do nothing. Whatever the-- wherever they would send us, we have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came back to Kiev, because Kiev was the capital. They gave us a street-- a train car, a big train car. We were 50 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they send us in groups. They send us to sing for the military, for the echelons that went to the war and the-- the poor soldiers that came already wounded. As I was a soloist, and my late husband was the-- the pianist, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they gave him an accordion. And we went as a team in one place. And the other were sent in different places. So we worked. At night, we-- we were taken from all those places back to the train car. And if we have got to go somewheres, they-- they-- they took the train car, and then joined it to-- to the-- to a train where it went in that direction that we have got to go this way. But now, during the war when we were still in Kiev, the Germans went further and further. And we were working, not knowing what will happen. And it started the bombardment. And the Germans came in to Kiev. And the Soviet army had a very hard nut to crack [? to push them. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was in 1941. And-- and we were there. We didn't know. We-- we saw the tanks come in with the Germans. And-- and they were shooting. And-- and-- in-- in the middle, we didn't know. We fell. We didn't know if it was a German-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. We fell. And when the Soviet Union-- when the Soviet soldier, army, pulled them back-- and it was a fight-- they came to collect their dead. And we were with my husband there. They picked us up. They took us to a field hospital. From there, with the chest, they feel that we are still alive. They send us right away to a hospital in Chelyabinsk. This is more rural. And-- and I never saw Chelyabinsk, the city. I was only in the hospital with my husband, where we were amputated. My husband was lucky. He wore a long sleeve. And he had enough tissues and skin to-- to make a nice, high amputation. But it was clean and fairly done. I had short sleeves. And my wound was very dirty. And it was a gangrene. And they started to cut and cut. And it was-- I was very, very sick. They have got to give me blood infusions. And-- and they amputated without-- without anesthesia. Because they didn't have it. They didn't have any-- any medicines. They didn't have soap to wash their scent. With scent, they washed their hands. And one nurse, she could see how I am suffering. She gave me poison, cyanide. She said, you'll-- it will take a second. You won't feel anything. But my-- my husband who was there, only a colleague and friend, he was a great optimist. He said, oh, no. You won't do anything. You are young. You will overcome it. And you'll see, we'll come home. You might find some-- find somebody from-- you have family. I'll find my wife. She will be a sister to you. And you won't do nothing. And thanks to him, he-- I am alive. He said, I arranged all my repertoire in one hand. He was a great pianist, a great musician. And-- and I didn't do anything. I took the pain. And I overcame it. The-- my colleagues from the philharmonic, from the choir, they didn't know where we were. We didn't know where they were. Because they haven't been in one place. But we had there very good friends. And-- and then they-- they tried to find where we are. And one girlfriend especially, Rosa Porozowska, she lived in Brazil. She passed away a few years ago. She did her utmost to find us. And she did find us in the hospital. We were-- a half a year we were away that we didn't know what will happen to us. And they didn't let us out from the hospital, because I was very useful. I started to make with one hand tampons from cotton to help the-- to sterilize and to help all the people who they brought from the war, wounded people. It was horrible. And then they connected the hospital. And we were found. So they sent also a colleague, a friend of ours from Lutsk. His name was Itsik Glazer. And he came to the hospital and took us out. And I must tell you, we made so much friends. And-- and the whole faculty of the doctors, they loved us. They gave us a farewell party. And they loved us very, very much. And we came to Omsk in Siberia. When you-- when you are talking, it freezes. [LAUGHS] When-- when you breathe, you can't breathe. It freezes. And then we came in there. And my friend went right away-- you couldn't go into a store and buy something. But on the-- on the market, like they call the black market, they brought [NON-ENGLISH]-- it means shoes that you can wear in the snow and the frost-- and underwear and one pair of mittens. For me, one, and for my husband, one. And the next day, we went, and we took part in the concert. And my husband sat down to the piano, and Weiner was directing. And-- and he was-- the tears was running down his cheeks. Luckily, he was with the back to the audience. He couldn't imagine that he will play like that with one hand. And that is the end of the story. We joined the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was in-- in '40-- still and '41, and the beginning of '42. And we were together then until '44. We came back to-- we was-- we were all over the Soviet Union, even in the-- in the-- in the far east. And we came back to Kiev. Because it was in 1944. And we lived through a pogrom in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you why. A Jew came back from the war with a lot of medals. And he was a hero. And he got the highest awards that a-- that a military man can. And he came in to his apartment. When he lived there, his apartment was-- but-- so he came in to the apartment where he lived. And he found there somebody else living, a gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, I'm sorry. This is my apartment. Now, we have to do something. He says, where did you buy those medals, in Tashkent, on the bazaar, on the market? So it took something, so he gave him a push or-- or-- he killed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a funeral. And it was, kill Jews. And this was the-- the dessert after Babi Yar, that 100,000 Jews were killed. And they said that all-- there were only Jews were killed in Babi Yar. And they never wrote and they never said that it was Jews. So, that's-- and we came there. And we saw it from a-- from our house where we lived, we saw what-- what was happening until it-- it was-- and then we lived-- we went-- the choir [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was resolved. And all the gentiles who came from Lutsk went back to Lutsk. They found their families, maybe richer than they were before. And we couldn't go back to Lutsk, because there was a grave of 18,000 where all our families or some other were killed. This-- my brother, Zeylik. I met one, a survivor. He said, oh, your brother went with the-- with the ones that went Thursday. It was a Thursday that they took 3,000 men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they brought them in a certain place, and they killed them all. You see? So we couldn't return to Lutsk. How did you find out about what happened to the families in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, through survivors, through people that-- that we met after the war. And we started to work in Kharkov also a big city in the Ukraine. And there was a well-known Ukrainian theater that we were working in the theater with great, great artists. I also have pictures from them. When we left in 1947, they-- they gave us pictures. We were very, very friendly. And they gave us pictures. And we left in 1947 as Polish citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left, how did you know the war had ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know the war had ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was in 1945. The war was ended. It was everywhere. The Soviets also-- also looked forward for the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were in Kharkov when the war was ended. And in 1946, because they knew that Poland will be a satellite-- oh, it will be more on their-- on their side. They have helped Poland very much. So that's why they let us out as Polish citizens before the war. And we came then to Stettin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we came in a time when the Soviets-- Stettin was a port that belonged to Poland 600 years ago. So the Soviets said that now it has to belong to Poland again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was Stettin the port, and Silesia, upper and down Silesia. They took away from Germany. They sent out all the Germans. And they brought in the outcasts from Poland, from the prisons. Do you remember the [? Mario ?] from Cuba, that they came, and Castro sent all those prisoners? This was the lesson that the Soviets gave-- gave-- gave Castro, how to send the prisoner. It started here in Miami. For me, it was deja vu.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they started about again to kill Jews. And in Kielce, was in August, was a pogrom. And when we came back, Weiner, the first, he went to Kielce. And we were so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't the time when it was the pogrom. And we couldn't-- we didn't see. We didn't know what happened. But luckily, that day he went out. But he couldn't come to Stettin, has got to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: So afterward, he came back. And then he married my girlfriend, Rivka. And they went to Warsaw. Because they-- they worked-- he was a teacher also. And he loved children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he lost a wife and a child, a girl. And he started to work with children. In Otwock, there was children who came from all over who were brought. And later, they were taken to Israel. And he worked with them. And then they went to Argentina to Buenos Aires, because she had a sister there. So now, they lived in Buenos Aires and also made a wonderful name for themselves. And we were also in contact, but we saw them very seldom. We-- we were once-- in 1964, we went to Brazil for to-- to appear for the pioneer women. And then we went already to Argentina, because my husband had a brother there, that-- whom he didn't see 35 years. So we combined to see the Weiners and his brother. And we gave a concert there. And we made a lot, a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in 1948 February 6, in Stettin. And this was a marriage-- you see, we went through so much together and also handicappedness. And we have got to manage life. And we were looking for his wife. And this Stettin, came in-- she was from Chelm. And she lived through-- it also a city in Poland. She lived through, in Chelm, the war with a child of five, Moishele. And there was a man, Mr. Brustman, who came in and met my husband and told him this story. He came to our house and told him this story, that he had a wife and three daughters. And he was a good tailor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Germans took him to work for them as a tailor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And his three daughters and wife were like everyone. Once, he-- he was taken out of his place where he was working. And he has got to look on how his wife, three daughters, my husband's wife with the child-- she was an opera singer. She held the child in her arms. And she held her head high and sang. And they led them into the street, into the main street of Chelm, and killed them one by one.
